journal-title-group.journal-title: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation

title-group.article-title: The effect of mace training on strength, flexibility, and stability in elite wrestlers: a randomized controlled trial

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/038pb1155grid.448691.60000 0004 0454 905XDepartment of Physiotherapy and Rehabilitation, Faculty of Health Sciences, Erzurum Technical University, Erzurum, Turkey
2https://ror.org/03je5c526grid.411445.10000 0001 0775 759XDepartment of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, Faculty of Medicine, Atatürk University, Erzurum, Turkey
3https://ror.org/038pb1155grid.448691.60000 0004 0454 905XFaculty of Sports Sciences, Erzurum Technical University, Erzurum, Turkey
4https://ror.org/01wntqw50grid.7256.60000 0001 0940 9118Faculty of Sports Sciences, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This study examined the impact of mace training on strength, flexibility, stabilization, and endurance in elite wrestlers, exploring its potential advantages over traditional resistance training.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A randomized controlled trial was conducted with 27 elite male wrestlers assigned to either a mace training group (n = 13) or a control group (n = 14). The experimental group performed mace exercises three times a week for 12 weeks, in addition to regular training. Pre- and post-training assessments included grip strength, shoulder flexibility, closed kinetic chain upper extremity stability (CKCUEST), push-up endurance, and proprioception assessed by shoulder joint position sense (JPS) testing using a digital inclinometer at 60° and 120°. Group × time interactions were analyzed using repeated measures ANOVA. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

sec.title: Results
Conclusion
sec.p: Mace exercises effectively enhance grip strength, flexibility, and stabilization in elite wrestlers, making them a valuable tool for functional training. Future research should explore long-term effects and applications in other combat sports.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: Our study should not be considered a clinical trial because it does not involve any medical or therapeutic intervention on any health or biological outcome. The study aims to evaluate athletic performance parameters only. Therefore, it does not require registration in the international clinical trial registry system.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s13102-026-01545-8.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Wrestling is a high-intensity combat sport demanding a unique blend of anaerobic power, muscular endurance, strength, and agility to respond to explosive exchanges and multi-phase grappling actions stabilization [1]. Success in wrestling is largely dependent on technical proficiency, but also on physical attributes such as grip strength, upper body power, and dynamic stabilization [2, 3]. Due to its complex physiological demands, various training methods have been explored to optimize performance and minimize injury risk in wrestlers [4]. One such emerging training approach is mace training, a historically rooted but functionally relevant resistance exercise regimen [5].

Mace training, rooted in ancient Indian and Persian warrior conditioning practices, traditionally utilized heavy clubs or maces to enhance upper extremity strength, rotational power, and grip endurance [6]. In contemporary strength and conditioning programs, mace training has gained recognition for its ability to improve shoulder flexibility, core stabilization, and proprioceptive awareness [7]. Given the high prevalence of shoulder demands in combat sports, incorporating exercises that enhance joint mobility and stability is essential for maximizing athletic performance [8]. Recent research underscores the growing importance of functional training methodologies in combat sports, highlighting that multi-joint, dynamic resistance exercises, such as those featured in mace training, offer more substantial benefits to athletic performance than traditional resistance training approaches [9]. Furthermore, grip strength is identified as a critical performance indicator in wrestling, directly contributing to the execution of takedowns, holds, and defensive maneuvers [10]. Since mace training predominantly engages grip endurance and upper body stability, it presents a promising supplementary training method for wrestlers, potentially bridging the gap between functional strength development and sport-specific performance demands.

Numerous biomechanical studies emphasize the critical role of shoulder flexibility and stabilization training in enhancing athletic performance and minimizing injury risk in athletes [11, 12]. Their findings are particularly relevant given the high upper extremity demands and injury incidence observed in wrestling and similar disciplines. Dynamic resistance exercises that incorporate controlled, circular motions characteristic of mace training have been shown to improve shoulder joint control and stability, as demonstrated by Myers et al. in their research on segmental stabilization training [13]. Similarly, Reed et al. highlighted that functional resistance exercises contribute to significant enhancements in joint proprioception, core stability, and neuromuscular efficiency in elite athletes [14].

Given its emphasis on joint flexibility, muscular endurance, and stabilization, mace training may help reduce risk factors commonly associated with shoulder, elbow, and wrist injuries in wrestlers. While current evidence directly linking mace training to injury prevention is limited, these exercise characteristics align with general principles of injury risk mitigation in combat sports [15]. Therefore, further longitudinal and injury surveillance studies are warranted to establish its efficacy in preventing musculoskeletal injuries. Furthermore, Tabben et al. emphasized that functional resistance exercises focusing on dynamic grip engagement and stabilization provide substantial benefits for combat athletes recovering from upper limb injuries [16].

One of the widely adopted functional training tools in modern wrestling is the Bulgarian bag, which was specifically developed to enhance dynamic strength, rotational power, and grip endurance among combat athletes. Bulgarian bag training has shown benefits in improving core strength, shoulder mobility, and grip strength, making it a popular choice for wrestling-specific conditioning [17, 18]. While the Bulgarian bag engages similar movement patterns to mace training (e.g., swinging, rotation, overhead work), the mace differs in its long lever, offset load, requiring greater shoulder stabilization and rotational control. Given these biomechanical differences, mace training may offer neuromuscular advantages; however, this remains speculative as direct comparative research is still lacking.

Mace training, originally rooted in ancient South Asian warrior conditioning systems, has undergone a renaissance in modern athletic development. Traditionally used by wrestlers and martial artists to build grip strength, shoulder endurance, and rotational control, the mace has conceptually influenced the design of other functional tools such as the kettlebell and Bulgarian bag [17, 19]. Contemporary sports science has embraced these offset-loaded implements due to their biomechanical efficiency in developing multiplanar strength, dynamic stabilisation, and proprioceptive awareness. Among them, the mace remains unique in its capacity to stimulate rotational torque, grip resilience, and shoulder girdle stability qualities, particularly critical in grappling-based sports like wrestling.

Despite its historical roots and growing recognition in functional training, empirical evidence on the direct impact of mace training on performance outcomes in combat sports remains limited. While traditional strength training methods are well-established, the specific effects of mace training on grip strength, rotational power, unilateral stability, and movement efficiency in wrestlers are underexplored. This study aims to address this gap by evaluating the influence of mace training on grip strength, upper limb endurance, flexibility, stabilization, and balance in elite wrestlers.

The study will provide practical insights for wrestling coaches, strength and conditioning specialists, and sports rehabilitation professionals, offering evidence-based recommendations for incorporating mace training into athletic development programs. Additionally, the research will contribute to a deeper understanding of the biomechanical and physiological adaptations associated with mace exercises, proposing future research directions to explore its long-term effects across various combat sports disciplines.
Therefore, the hypotheses of this study are as follows:

list-item.p: H1: Mace training will result in moderate-to-large improvements in grip strength, upper extremity endurance, and shoulder flexibility in elite wrestlers compared to traditional training alone.
H2: Mace exercises will produce moderate-to-large enhancements in stabilization and proprioception relative to traditional training methods.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: This study employed a randomized controlled trial research design to examine the effects of incorporating mace training into traditional wrestling programs on upper extremity strength, flexibility, stabilization, balance, grip strength, and cardiovascular endurance. A pre-test post-test control group design was utilized, with participants randomly assigned to experimental and control groups through a computer-generated randomization process. To maintain group equivalence, block randomization was applied, allocating participants in blocks of four to minimize potential baseline differences. An independent researcher who was not involved in participant recruitment, assessment, or training prepared the randomization sequence. Group assignments were concealed using sealed, opaque, sequentially numbered envelopes, which were opened only after the baseline assessments were completed. This approach ensured allocation concealment and minimized selection bias. The experimental group engaged in mace exercises alongside traditional training for 12 weeks, whereas the control group adhered exclusively to their traditional training regimen.

The control group continued their usual national team wrestling programme, which consisted of three weekly technical-tactical sessions and two weekly resistance training sessions. The resistance training involved classical compound lifts (bench press, deadlifts, and back squats) performed at 3–4 sets of 6–10 repetitions at 70–85% of one-repetition maximum (1RM). Aerobic conditioning was also included as part of their routine. Unlike the mace training group, the control group’s training intensity and volume remained constant throughout the 12 weeks and did not follow a progressive overload model (Fig. 1).

caption.p: Study desing of the whole protocol

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: The G*Power software was used to determine the required sample size based on a repeated measures ANOVA for within-between interactions (F tests). The analysis parameters included an effect size (f) of 0.25, alpha (α) of 0.05, power (1-β) of 0.90, two groups, and two measurements with a 0.7 correlation between repeated measures. The power analysis indicated that 28 participants were required to achieve statistical significance; however, to account for potential data loss, the study initially included 30 wrestlers. During the intervention period, one participant sustained an injury during a training competition (n = 1), and two participants attended a national training camp (n = 2), preventing them from completing the study. Consequently, the study was finalized with 27 participants (14 in the Wrestling-Only Group and 13 in the Wrestling + Mace Exercises Group) (Fig. 2). Therefore, the study included 27 elite male wrestlers (aged 18–30) competing at the national and international levels, certified by the Turkish Wrestling Federation. Inclusion criteria required at least three years of regular training experience and a minimum of three training sessions per week. Wrestlers with orthopedic or metabolic disorders were excluded.

sec.title: Mace exercise protocol
sec.p: Participants in the experimental group engaged in mace training three times per week for 12 weeks, following their traditional training sessions. Each session lasted 15 min and included 4 sets of 10–12 repetitions per exercise, with 60–90 s of rest between sets. The mace weight was standardized to 20% of each athlete’s body weight, a load that provided the best balance between movement control and strength stimulus during pilot testing. This percentage was determined through expert consultation with a Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist (CSCS) with over ten years of experience in functional training and combat athlete preparation, combined with pilot testing on three national-level wrestlers and two strength coaches. The pilot trials demonstrated that loads below 15% of body weight did not provide sufficient stimulus, while loads above 25% compromised movement quality. Based on these findings, 20% of body weight was established as the optimal and safe training load. The 20% body weight load was manageable across dynamic, rotational movements while still producing significant neuromuscular demand. Our pilot testing confirmed that lower weights (< 15% BW) lacked sufficient stimulus, while higher weights (> 25% BW) compromised form and range of motion. This observation was based on a small internal pilot pre-test conducted with three national-level wrestlers and two strength coaches prior to the study. These sessions aimed to optimize mace loading relative to dynamic control and safety. The pilot was not designed as a formal or publishable study but guided protocol development. The expert consulted was a certified strength and conditioning specialist (CSCS) with over ten years of experience in functional training and combat athlete preparation, particularly in using unconventional training tools such as maces and clubbells.

The mace training protocol was delivered three times per week for 12 weeks. Exercises targeted shoulder flexibility, grip strength, and core-shoulder stability. Training progression followed a periodised model. Weeks 1–4 focused on bilateral movements with basic patterns (e.g., frontal and transverse swings, uppercuts). Weeks 5–8 introduced increased volume (4–5 sets) and added unilateral variations. Weeks 9–12 integrated multiplanar, dynamic movements with grip variations and complex sequences (e.g., 360 swings, offset presses). Repetition ranges progressed from 10 to 12 in early weeks to 15–20 in later stages. Rest intervals were gradually shortened to increase metabolic demand. The mace load was set at 20% body weight and remained consistent to prioritise movement quality.

The mace training protocol incorporated a range of functional exercises designed to target multiple aspects of athletic performance. These exercises included 360-degree swings, aimed at enhancing shoulder flexibility and grip strength, and 10-to-2 swings, which focused on developing rotational power. The Mace Front Press was utilized to improve overhead stability, while the Mace Offset Squat engaged the core and lower body strength, promoting balance under uneven load conditions. Additionally, the Mace Rotational Lunge was implemented to enhance unilateral stability and dynamic balance. To address asymmetrical strength and loading adaptations, Mace Single-Arm Swings were included in the training regimen. All exercises were meticulously performed under professional supervision to ensure adherence to proper technique and to minimize the risk of injury (Fig. 3).

The control group followed a traditional wrestling training programme that included three weekly sessions of technical and tactical wrestling drills and two weekly resistance training sessions. The resistance sessions involved classical compound lifts such as bench press, deadlifts, and back squats performed at 3–4 sets of 6–10 repetitions at 70–85% of one-repetition maximum (1RM). The training did not include any use of functional tools, rotational implements, or mace-like equipment. All training sessions were conducted under the supervision of certified strength and conditioning coaches, consistent with typical off-season training preparation for wrestlers. Unlike the experimental group, the control group’s training volume and intensity remained constant throughout the 12 weeks and did not follow a progressive overload model. This ensured that any differences observed between groups could be attributed to the progressive and functional nature of the mace training intervention.

sec.title: Anthropometric measurements
sec.p: Height and Body Weight: Height and body weight measurements were taken using a portable stadiometer (Holtain Ltd.) and a bioelectrical impedance device (Tanita BC-310). Height measurements were conducted with participants standing barefoot, in an upright position, with their heads aligned in the Frankfurt plane. Body weight measurements were taken in the morning after an overnight fast of at least 8 h, with participants instructed to refrain from food and caloric beverages after 10:00 p.m. the previous evening. Only plain water was permitted until measurement. All measurements were performed at the same time window (08:00–09:00 a.m.) using the same calibrated bioelectrical impedance device under standardized conditions.

sec.title: Hand grip strength
sec.p: Grip strength was assessed using a Hand Grip Dynamometer (Takei A5001). Grip strength was measured bilaterally on both the dominant and non-dominant hands using a hand dynamometer, and the maximum force was exerted. The test was performed in a seated position with the shoulder adducted and neutrally rotated, elbow flexed at 90°, forearm in neutral position, and wrist between 0° and 30° extension, following standard hand dynamometry protocols [20]. Three trials were conducted, with a two-minute rest interval between each attempt, and the highest value was recorded. Grip strength is a crucial parameter for wrestlers, as many wrestling techniques require a strong grip [21]. This test is an effective tool for evaluating upper extremity muscle endurance and strength.

sec.title: Shoulder internal and external rotator strength
sec.p: The strength of the shoulder internal and external rotator muscles was measured using an isometric dynamometer (LaFayette). Internal and external rotational strength were assessed separately for both dominant and non-dominant shoulders. Participants pressed or pulled against the device with maximum force while maintaining a fixed position [22]. The test was conducted three times, and the highest value was recorded for analysis. This measurement is critical for assessing shoulder stabilization and strength, which are essential for wrestling performance.

sec.title: Push-up test
sec.p: Participants maintained standard push-up form: hands shoulder-width apart, trunk aligned, and full elbow extension required to count each repetition. Incomplete or improperly aligned repetitions were excluded. Participants performed as many push-ups as possible within one minute to assess upper-body endurance. Proper push-up form was strictly maintained throughout the test, and the maximum number of repetitions completed was recorded as strength endurance [23].

sec.title: Shoulder flexibility
sec.p: Shoulder range of motion was assessed using a measuring tape. For the test, participants stood with their feet together and head upright. To assess internal and external rotation flexibility, the seventh cervical vertebra (C7) spinous process and the fifth thoracic vertebra (T5) spinous process were identified as reference points.

sec.title: External rotator muscle flexibility
sec.p: Participants were instructed to move their arm behind their neck with the thumb pointing downward, performing maximum external rotation. The distance between the tip of the thumb and the T5 vertebra was measured using a measuring tape on the dominant side.

sec.title: Internal rotator muscle flexibility
sec.p: Participants were asked to move their arm behind their lower back with the thumb pointing upward, performing maximum internal rotation. The distance between the tip of the thumb and the C7 vertebra was measured on the dominant side. Each test was repeated three times, and the best value was recorded [24].

sec.title: Closed kinetic chain upper extremity stability test (CKCUEST)
sec.p: This test was used to evaluate upper extremity stabilization and coordination capacity. Participants assumed a push-up position, maintaining their bodies parallel to the ground. For 15 s, they repeatedly lifted one hand and touched the opposite hand. The number of touches performed within the time limit was recorded, with the highest performance value used for evaluation [25]. This test is a key indicator of upper extremity stabilization, which is essential for wrestling.

sec.title: Proprioception
sec.p: Proprioception was assessed through joint position sense (JPS) testing, which evaluates an individual’s ability to reproduce predefined joint angles without visual feedback. The test was conducted using a digital inclinometer with a sensitivity of ± 0.1°, ensuring precise angle measurement. Participants were seated in a standardized position to minimize compensatory movements. The shoulder and elbow joints were tested at 60° and 120°, corresponding to functionally relevant ranges in combat sports movements. Proprioception testing at 60° and 120° shoulder elevation was performed for both the dominant and non-dominant limbs. Each participant performed three trials per angle, with a 10-second interval between repetitions. The absolute error (AE) between the target and reproduced angles was recorded and averaged across trials. To maintain consistency, all tests were conducted by the same examiner. Participants were blindfolded during the test to eliminate visual input, and verbal cues were kept minimal to avoid external influence [26]. Rebolledo et al., who reported excellent reliability for shoulder joint position sense assessments using a digital inclinometer, with ICC values between 0.93 and 0.97 at 60° and 120° abduction [27].

The angles of 60° and 120° were selected based on their functional relevance to wrestling-specific shoulder positions during clinching, lifting, and defensive maneuvers. These ranges reflect common movement amplitudes used in upper limb engagement during combat tasks and have been used in prior proprioception research in overhead and combat athletes [27].

Joint position sense (JPS) was assessed using an active-to-active reproduction method wherein participants were first instructed to actively position their limb at the target angle with the help of visual feedback, then returned to the neutral position and asked to actively replicate the angle without visual feedback. This method was chosen to reflect functional movement control.
Only absolute error (AE) was reported in this study, as AE provides a global measure of accuracy regardless of error direction.

All assessments were conducted by an experienced physiotherapist who was blinded to group allocation but was aware of the assessment timing (i.e., pre- or post-intervention). The assessor was not involved in the intervention delivery or data analysis and had over fifteen years of experience in musculoskeletal testing. Attendance was recorded for both groups. Weekly training logs were collected and reviewed to ensure compliance. No major deviations were observed in either group.

sec.title: Ethics
sec.p: Ethical approval was granted by the Erzurum Technical University Scientific Research and Publication Ethics Committee (Meeting No: 04, Decision No: 29, Date: 30.03.2023), and the study complied with the Declaration of Helsinki. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Sample size estimation was conducted using G*Power software (Version 3.1.9.7, Düsseldorf, Germany). A repeated measures ANOVA (within-between interaction, F-test family) was used, with the following parameters: effect size (f) = 0.25, alpha level (α) = 0.05, statistical power (1 − β) = 0.90, two groups, and two measurement points, assuming a correlation of 0.7 between repeated measures. The effect size (f = 0.25) used in the power analysis was chosen based on Cohen’s conventional criteria for a medium effect size. This estimate is consistent with prior literature evaluating the impact of functional resistance training on performance outcomes in athletes [28, 29] and was deemed appropriate given the limited availability of prior mace training data. Only complete cases were included in the statistical analysis. Participants who did not complete the full intervention or post-assessment (n = 3) were excluded prior to analysis. No missing data imputation was performed, and all analyses were conducted on the final sample of 27 participants with complete data across all variables.

All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS software (Version 28.0, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Baseline comparisons between groups were analyzed using independent samples t-tests, with effect sizes (Cohen’s d) computed [30] and classified according to Hopkins’ thresholds [31] (d < 0.2 = trivial, 0.2–0.59 = small, 0.6–1.19 = moderate, 1.2–1.99 = large, 2.0–3.99 = very large and > 4.0 = nearly perfect). To assess the effects of mace training over time, a two-way repeated measures ANOVA (group × time interaction) was conducted, comparing the mace training group and the control group across pre- and post-intervention measurements. Effect sizes for ANOVA were reported as partial eta squared (η²), with thresholds of 0.01 (small), 0.06 (moderate), and 0.14 (large). Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05 for all analyses.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The baseline demographic characteristics of the participants, including age, height, weight, and training experience, demonstrated no significant differences between the mace training group and the control group, indicating balanced allocation of participants. Statistical analyses revealed non-significant differences in age (p = 0.352, d = 0.34, small effect size), height (p = 0.689, d = 0.16, trivial effect size), weight (p = 0.451, d = 0.24, small effect size), and training experience (p = 0.617, d = 0.22, small effect size) between the groups. These results suggest that the groups were well-matched at baseline (Table 1).

caption.p: Demographic characteristics of the elite wrestlers

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation
ES Effect size, (Effect sizes (d) were calculated using Cohen’s d formula and classified according to Hopkins’ criteria), SES Small Effect Size, TES Trivial Effect Size

sec.p: Dominant and non-dominant hand grip strength (HGS) demonstrated significant improvements over time in both the mace training and control groups (Dominant HGS: F [1, 25] = 47.75, p < 0.001, η² = 0.62, large; Non-dominant HGS: F [1, 25] = 14.68, p = 0.01, η² = 0.34, large). No significant overall group differences were observed for dominant (F [1, 25] = 0.17, p = 0.68, η² = 0.01, trivial) or non-dominant HGS (F [1, 25] = 0.09, p = 0.76, η² = 0.01, trivial). However, significant group × time interaction effects were found (Dominant HGS: F [1, 25] = 28.01, p < 0.001, η² = 0.53, large; Non-dominant HGS: F [1, 25] = 12.86, p < 0.05, η² = 0.34, large), favoring the mace training group.

For shoulder flexibility, dominant external rotator flexibility showed significant increases over time (F [1, 25] = 21.62, p < 0.001, η² = 0.41, large), with no significant group differences (F [1, 25] = 1.05, p = 0.32, η² = 0.04, small). Yet, the significant interaction effect (F [1, 25] = 17.08, p < 0.001, η² = 0.41, large) indicated more pronounced improvements in the mace group. Conversely, non-dominant internal rotator flexibility also improved significantly over time (F [1, 25] = 114.46, p < 0.001, η² = 0.79, large), but did not show significant differences between groups (F [1, 25] = 0.36, p = 0.55, η² = 0.02, small) or significant interaction effects (F [1, 25] = 0.50, p = 0.49, η² = 0.03, small), suggesting similar progress in both groups. These findings indicate that while both training modalities contributed to improvements in grip strength and shoulder flexibility, the mace training protocol provided additional benefits, particularly in enhancing dominant limb performance and promoting greater gains in flexibility (Table 2).

caption.p: Hand grip strength (HGS) and shoulder flexibility performance scores of wrestlers

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation

HGS(D) Dominant Hand Grip Strength, HGS(ND)Non-Dominant Hand Grip Strength, Shoulder Mobility(D)Dominant Shoulder External Rotator Mobility, Shoulder Mobility(ND), Non-dominant Shoulder Internal Rotator Mobility

caption.p: Upper extremity stability, rotator muscle strength, and Push-Up performance scores of wrestlers

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation

CKCUEST Closed Kinetic Chain Upper Extremity Stability Test, rep.num Repetition number, IRS(D)Dominant internal rotational strength, IRS(ND) Non-dominant internal rotational strength, ERS(D)Dominant external rotational strength, ERS(ND) Non-dominant external rotational strength

caption.p: Proprioception scores at 60° and 120° angles of elite wrestlers

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation
D Dominant, ND Non-dominant ES Effect size
* There is significant interaction between group*time (p < 0,05)

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To our knowledge, there are no prior randomized trials evaluating a mace-specific training program in elite wrestlers. Related work using cognate implements has shown improvements in shoulder range of motion and rotator strength in other athlete groups (e.g., Indian clubbell programs in handball players [32] and cricket fast bowlers [33]), and Bulgarian bag protocols have improved upper-body performance in wrestlers [17]; our trial extends this literature by testing a mace-based intervention in an elite wrestling cohort and by assessing grip strength, shoulder mobility, and upper-extremity stability. The findings indicate that incorporating mace exercises into traditional wrestling training produces significant group × time interaction effects in upper extremity strength, grip strength, shoulder flexibility, stabilization, and muscular endurance. These results demonstrate that mace training led to greater improvements compared to traditional training alone. However, proprioception assessments at 60° and 120° showed no significant group differences. These findings suggest that while mace exercises emphasize dynamic stabilization and functional strength, they may not provide the specific neurosensory stimulus required to enhance joint position sense. As such, improvements in proprioceptive control may require more targeted interventions such as perturbation-based training or neuromuscular facilitation rather than general functional strength exercises. Therefore, although mace training may support joint control indirectly, its isolated effect on proprioceptive accuracy appears limited based on the current data.

These findings are broadly consistent with prior work in related populations rather than directly comparable trials in elite wrestlers. For example, Zawadka et al. (2024) examined individuals engaged in recreational strength training and reported positive associations between upper-limb functional assessments and shoulder mobility/posture; although not an intervention study nor in wrestlers, the direction of evidence aligns with our observed improvements. Our randomized trial extends this literature by testing a mace-based intervention in an elite wrestling cohort [34]. Mace exercises, known for their ability to activate hand and forearm musculature, have been shown to enhance grip strength, which supports our findings regarding dominant and non-dominant hand grip strength gains. Similarly, Zuo et al. (2022) demonstrated that functional training effectively improves upper extremity muscular endurance and coordination, corroborating the improvements in endurance observed in our study [28]. Furthermore, Tural et al. (2021) investigated the effects of an eight-week core training program on maximal strength, vertical jump, and flexibility in freestyle wrestlers, emphasizing its role in postural stabilization, balance, and explosive power development [35]. Given that mace exercises engage multiple muscle groups involved in dynamic movement, particularly those responsible for upper extremity strength, shoulder flexibility, and stabilization, their application in wrestling-specific training appears functionally relevant. Like core training, mace exercises involve multiplanar movements, which likely contribute to improvements in balance, stability, and force production. Considering that wrestling requires complex, high-force actions such as pushing, pulling, and explosive movements, the increased shoulder stabilization, grip strength, and core control developed through mace training may offer significant performance benefits.

Our study identified significant improvements in shoulder flexibility, which align with previous findings emphasizing the benefits of dynamic resistance training for joint stabilization and flexibility enhancement. Salles et al. (2015) demonstrated that dynamic resistance exercises contribute to improved shoulder stabilization and flexibility in athletes [29], while Andersen et al. (2010) highlighted the advantages of functional training in promoting joint stability [36], making it particularly relevant for sports requiring high levels of upper extremity control, such as wrestling. In the present study, improvements in shoulder flexibility were observed bilaterally, suggesting that the dynamic and cyclic nature of mace exercises effectively engages the surrounding musculature and enhances joint range of motion. Furthermore, preparatory physical training has been shown to significantly improve athletes’ flexibility levels [37], a crucial factor for optimizing performance and mitigating injury risk in wrestling, which demands extensive dynamic movement capacity. The circular and wide-ranging motion patterns characteristic of mace exercises may facilitate gains in flexibility, stabilization, and alignment, aligning with previous research that underscores the importance of functional training in enhancing movement efficiency. This study extends the existing literature by examining the integration of mace exercises into preparatory training programs and their role in improving flexibility, mobility, and stabilization in wrestlers. These findings are consistent with previous work demonstrating that targeted upper limb functional training improves shoulder muscle balance and peak torque output, which are essential in overhead-dominant sports such as wrestling [38].

Our findings revealed significant improvements in upper extremity stabilization, as indicated by the Closed Kinetic Chain Upper Extremity Stability Test (CKCUEST) in the experimental group (p < 0.001). These results align with prior research demonstrating the benefits of functional resistance training in enhancing muscle balance and stabilization [39]. The ability of mace exercises to improve upper extremity stabilization, particularly in closed kinetic chain movements, further supports their relevance for wrestling, where stability is essential for performance. Additionally, proprioceptive error scores at the 120° angle were higher in the intervention group compared to the control group for both dominant and non-dominant extremities. This finding is consistent with evidence suggesting that proprioceptive training enhances somatosensory and sensorimotor functions [40, 41]. Proprioceptive-targeted training has been reported to yield greater improvements in joint position sense and motor performance compared to non-targeted interventions. In this context, the results of the present study suggest that mace exercises may provide proprioceptive benefits, contributing to the development of neuromuscular control and movement efficiency in wrestlers. From a practical standpoint, the improvements observed in grip strength and shoulder flexibility are highly relevant for wrestling performance, as these attributes directly affect an athlete’s ability to secure and maintain holds, perform clinch maneuvers, and execute high-force pulling and pushing techniques. Enhanced upper extremity stability and endurance, as evidenced by improved CKCUEST and push-up results, may translate into better control during prolonged grappling exchanges and more efficient force transfer during offensive and defensive actions. These neuromuscular adaptations could offer a competitive advantage in high-intensity match scenarios, underscoring the value of mace training as a sport-specific conditioning method.

Push-up capacity is a valid indicator of upper body muscular endurance and core stabilization, both of which are essential during sustained pushing, hand-fighting, and defensive sprawling techniques. Enhanced push-up performance may contribute to an athlete’s ability to maintain arm integrity during prolonged clinch situations, resist fatigue during offensive sequences, and generate repeated forceful upper-body contractions during high-tempo exchanges common in wrestling competition. The inclusion of mace exercises contributed to enhanced upper-body endurance and stabilization, leading to greater push-up performance among athletes [7]. These results support the notion that mace exercises serve as an effective modality for functional strength development in wrestlers, particularly in enhancing upper extremity endurance and neuromuscular stabilization. Compared to other functional training modalities, mace exercises provide distinct advantages, particularly in shoulder flexibility and upper body stabilization. While training methods incorporating kettlebells and resistance bands also yield benefits, each modality possesses unique biomechanical and physiological characteristics.

Despite notable gains in strength and flexibility, the mace training group did not exhibit statistically significant improvements in proprioception (joint position sense at 60° and 120°). This may be attributed to the nature of the intervention, which prioritised dynamic rotational control and global shoulder stability over fine-tuned neurosensory feedback. Proprioceptive adaptations particularly those measured through JPS tests often require targeted stimuli such as perturbation training or specific neuromuscular activation strategies. The mace’s training load may have been insufficiently precise to elicit measurable changes in sensorimotor accuracy for isolated joint angles. The unexpectedly higher proprioceptive error at 120° in the mace group may be attributed to the nature of the training stimulus, which emphasized dynamic, swinging, and rotational movements rather than precise joint repositioning at elevated angles. Given that most mace exercises do not target controlled abduction at or beyond 120°, neuromuscular adaptations in that range may have been insufficient. Furthermore, the eccentric and stabilizing demands imposed by mace movements could have introduced localized fatigue or altered muscle spindle sensitivity, affecting position sense at more extreme joint angles.

Despite these encouraging findings, several limitations warrant consideration. First, the sample was modest in size (n = 27) and restricted to elite male wrestlers from a single training environment; therefore, the precision of the estimated effects is limited and generalisability to female wrestlers, younger athletes, and different competitive levels should be made cautiously. The limited sample may also have reduced statistical power for some outcomes, particularly sensorimotor measures such as joint position sense. Second, the intervention lasted 12 weeks and no follow-up or detraining assessment was performed, so the durability of the observed adaptations remains unknown. Third, only one mace-training dose and loading strategy was tested, and we did not examine dose-response relationships or compare mace training with other offset-loaded or functional modalities (e.g., Bulgarian bag, kettlebell, clubbell); thus, the optimal prescription and the relative advantage of the mace over alternative approaches cannot be determined from this trial. Fourth, while we included widely used field and laboratory outcomes (grip strength, rotator strength, flexibility, CKCUEST, push-up endurance), we did not assess match-related performance indicators, movement quality on the mat, or injury incidence; accordingly, transfer to competition outcomes and potential injury-risk implications should not be inferred. Finally, although the assessor was blinded to group allocation, awareness of the assessment timing (pre/post) and the inherent variability of some measures (e.g., tape-based flexibility) may have introduced a small risk of measurement and learning effects. Future studies with larger, more diverse samples, preregistered protocols, longer follow-up periods, and comparative/dose-response designs are needed to confirm these findings and refine evidence-based mace-training prescriptions.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Integrating a 12-week mace-training programme (three sessions per week) into elite wrestlers’ usual training produced meaningful improvements in key upper-body capacities relevant to wrestling, including grip strength, shoulder rotator strength, shoulder flexibility, closed-chain upper-extremity stability, and muscular endurance, compared with traditional training alone. In contrast, joint position sense at 60° and 120° did not improve, suggesting that mace work by itself may not provide the angle-specific sensorimotor stimulus required to enhance proprioceptive accuracy and may need to be paired with targeted perturbation or neuromuscular-control training when proprioception is a primary goal. Taken together, the findings support the mace as a practical, sport-relevant adjunct for developing multiplanar strength and stability in the shoulder–grip complex of wrestlers; however, confirmation in larger and more diverse cohorts and evaluation of transfer to match performance and injury-related outcomes are still required.

def.p: Closed Kinetic Chain Upper Extremity Stability Test
Statistical Package for the Social Sciences

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We would like to extend our gratitude to all the elite wrestlers and their coaches for their dedication and cooperation in this study. Their contributions were essential to the successful completion of this research.

sec.title: CONSORT Compliance Statement
sec.p: This study adheres to the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (CONSORT) guidelines to ensure comprehensive and transparent reporting of the randomized controlled trial. The study design, methodology, participant flow, and statistical analysis conform to the CONSORT statement. A completed CONSORT checklist has been included as an additional supplementary file for reference.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: **EA** : Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Collection, Statistical Analysis, Writing – Original Draft. **BÖ:** Supervision, Investigation, Review & Editing. **CG** : Data analysis, Data Collection, Review & Editing. **SU** : Data analysis, Investigation, Validation, Review & Editing. **SÖ** : Supervision, Project Administration, Review & Editing. **MGK** : Conceptualization, Interpretation, Review & Editing. **MT** : Writing – Review & Editing, Data Collection. **GY** : Formal Analysis, Validation, Review & Editing.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This project numbered 2023-11 was supported by Erzurum Technical University scientific research project fund.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was approved by the Erzurum Technical University Scientific Research and Publication Ethics Committee (Meeting No: 04, Decision No: 29, Date: 30.03.2023) and was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.”

notes.title: Consent for publication
notes.p: Informed consent was obtained from all participants.”

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
